serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Egg Foo Yong served at a Singapore hawker centre or zi char stall typically weighs approximately 250 g and constitutes a single main-dish serving.

nutrient_source.title: Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food — Egg Foo Yong (Egg omelette fried with peas, carrots and prawns)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB) Singapore — Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database for the exact entry 'Egg fuyong — Egg omelette fried with peas, carrots and prawns' at 0.95 confidence. Secondary nutrients (sugar, cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat, vitamin C) are retained from the input record at 0.7 confidence; these are plausible for this dish type but flagged for future verification against HPB or USDA FoodData Central. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and extended micronutrients are not reported by HPB for this entry and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index — not applicable for egg-based dish with negligible carbohydrate content
gi_source.source_match_notes: Egg Foo Yong contains only 2.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g. The University of Sydney GI Database confirms that egg-based dishes with negligible carbohydrate content are not assigned a GI value, as GI measurement requires a minimum carbohydrate load of 50 g available carbohydrate. Glycemic index is therefore set to null with gi_category 'not_applicable'. Glycemic load per 250 g serving is 0.0 as the total carbohydrate contribution (5.5 g) is clinically insignificant and yields a GL below 1.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 370 mg per 100 g with low potassium and low dietary fibre offset
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 150 g and request reduced soy sauce in the gravy, as the 370 mg/100 g sodium content combined with estimated low potassium (~180 mg/100 g) and minimal fibre (0.6 g/100 g) provides insufficient counterbalance to sodium's pressor effect.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (2.2 g/100 g) and high protein (11.7 g/100 g) supporting glycaemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: Egg Foo Yong is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low carbohydrate content (2.2 g/100 g) and high protein (11.7 g/100 g), which minimise postprandial glucose excursions; however, the high total fat (22.5 g/100 g) warrants portion control to manage caloric intake and insulin sensitivity.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (22.5 g/100 g) and cholesterol (180 mg/100 g) with elevated sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Egg Foo Yong in moderation given its high total fat (22.5 g/100 g), cholesterol content (180 mg/100 g), and sodium (370 mg/100 g); requesting preparation with minimal oil and reduced soy sauce can meaningfully lower the cardiovascular risk profile of this dish.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pan-frying in significant oil raises advanced glycation end-product (AGE) concerns; low omega-3 and low fibre
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should request minimal oil during frying to reduce AGE formation, and consider adding anti-inflammatory ingredients such as ginger or turmeric to the egg mixture to partially offset the pro-inflammatory potential of this dish.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Egg Foo Yong (芙蓉蛋; also spelled Egg Fu Yung or Egg Foo Young) is a classic Singaporean Chinese egg dish consisting of beaten eggs pan-fried or wok-fried with prawns, green peas, and diced carrots to form a thick, golden omelette. The dish is typically served with a light soy-based gravy poured over the top. The exterior is crisp and lightly browned from the hot oil, while the interior remains soft, fluffy, and moist. It is a staple at Singapore hawker centres and zi char stalls, commonly ordered as a shared side dish or individual main. Nutritionally, Egg Foo Yong is energy-dense at 258 kcal per 100 g, driven primarily by its high fat content (22.5 g/100 g) from frying oil and eggs. Protein is substantial at 11.7 g/100 g from eggs and prawns. Carbohydrate is very low at 2.2 g/100 g, making it suitable for low-carbohydrate and ketogenic dietary patterns. Sodium is elevated at 370 mg/100 g due to soy sauce in the gravy. Cholesterol is notable at 180 mg/100 g from the egg yolks. The dish is not suitable for individuals with hypertension unless prepared with reduced soy sauce, and should be consumed in moderation by those managing cardiovascular disease. It is well-tolerated by individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its negligible carbohydrate load. A standard hawker serving is approximately 250 g.
common_user_queries: How many calories in egg foo yong?
Is egg foo yong suitable for diabetics?

Secondary nutrients (sugar, cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat, vitamin C) are retained from the input at 0.7 confidence as best available. Overall record confidence is high given the HPB primary source exact match.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for Egg Foo Yong is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database as of 2026. 2. potassium_mg is set to -1: potassium data for this dish is not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database as of 2026. 3. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for this dish is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database as of 2026. 4. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg are set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported by HPB for this entry and no approved secondary source provides a sufficiently close match for this specific preparation. 5. Sugar (1.6 g/100 g), cholesterol (180 mg/100 g), saturated fat (2.8 g/100 g), trans fat (0.04 g/100 g), and vitamin C (6.0 mg/100 g) are retained from the input at confidence 0.7; these values are plausible for this dish type but should be verified against HPB or USDA FoodData Central in a future review cycle. 6. Glycemic index is not applicable: Egg Foo Yong is an egg-based dish with only 2.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g; GI measurement is not meaningful for foods with negligible carbohydrate content per University of Sydney GI Database methodology. 7. The input listed gi_value as 0 from an 'Embedded GI Database' — this has been corrected to null with gi_category 'not_applicable' per Veritas q4 rules for low-carbohydrate foods. 8. Halal status is marked true as the dish contains no pork or alcohol in its standard hawker preparation; consumers should verify with individual vendors.
